key_departures.impact: Etienne was the unquestioned RB1, rushing for 1,107 yards, 7 TDs, and adding 292 receiving yards and 6 receiving TDs in 2025 — 1,399 total scrimmage yards and 13 TDs. The Jaguars could not match the Saints' 4yr/$52M offer due to cap constraints. The departure leaves a significant void in both early-down carries and the pass game out of the backfield.

Lloyd was the Jaguars' defensive engine in 2025 — second-team All-Pro, first Pro Bowl, 81 tackles, career-high 5 interceptions, 1.5 sacks, 10 QB hits. The Jaguars declined his fifth-year option and he hit free agency after a breakout season. Internal replacement is projected to be Ventrell Miller, a significant step down. Creates a genuine need at WLB.

Newsome started 11 games after being acquired via trade from Cleveland in October 2025. He was the CB2/CB3 in Jacksonville's rotation. His departure is partially offset by the retention of Montaric Brown and the existing presence of Travis Hunter, Jarrian Jones, and Jourdan Lewis. CB depth is thinner but the starting two are locked in.

Wingard was a 7-year Jaguars veteran who worked from undrafted free agent into a starting safety. His departure leaves Eric Murray and Antonio Johnson as the starting safety duo, with rookie Caleb Ransaw (missed 2025 on IR) in the mix. Considered a depth/rotational loss at the starter level — the safety group has continuity without him.
key_departures.comp_pick_projection: Unclear; one-year deal structure may limit comp formula impact

key_departures.impact: Brown was a depth/rotational receiver. Departure noted but minimal impact given depth at WR with Brian Thomas Jr., Jakobi Meyers, Travis Hunter, and Tim Patrick.

Patrick was acquired via trade from Detroit in the 2025 training camp and contributed as a veteran depth receiver. His departure reduces WR depth behind the top three but does not create a critical need.
Released to create $2.7M in cap space. Was backup TE behind Brenton Strange. Depth loss only.

signings.analysis: Brown had a breakout 2025 campaign — 12 starts, team-high 12 PBUs, 2 INTs, 69.6 passer rating allowed (6th-best in NFL among CBs with 200+ coverage snaps). The 3yr/$31.8M deal with $20.65M guaranteed is a legitimate investment, locking him in alongside Travis Hunter as the two starting outside CBs. Per Spotrac, the combined cap hit of Jaguars top-4 CBs in 2026 is just $22.6M — extraordinary value. CB2 need is fully resolved. CB depth is still a consideration given Newsome's departure but the starter-level need is gone.

Rodriguez rushed for 500 yards and 6 TDs on 112 carries in 2025 for Washington. The Jaguars project a committee with Rodriguez and second-year Bhayshul Tuten as the 1A/1B duo, with LeQuint Allen as the third-down back. Rodriguez's connection to HC Liam Coen (OC at Kentucky in 2021 during Rodriguez's best college season) adds scheme fit confidence. However, Rodriguez is not a bell-cow back, lacks significant pass-catching production, and is being asked to step into the role vacated by Etienne — a significant upgrade challenge. 2yr/$10M with $6.2M guaranteed is a starter-tier deal for RB market ($5M AAV is solidly in starter range). Need is PARTIALLY addressed: committee approach leaves open the possibility of drafting a RB to take the long-term lead role.

Gardeck is a pure pass-rush specialist LB who had 2.5 sacks, 6 TFLs, 25 pressures (6th among all LBs) on just 39% of defensive snaps in 2025. The 2yr/$6.5M re-sign is excellent value for his specific role. However, Gardeck does NOT fill the every-down coverage void left by Devin Lloyd's departure — his role is situational. The WLB starter gap remains open, with Ventrell Miller as the current projection. LB need remains elevated.

Morris is valued primarily as a blocker and special teamer. He caught 6 passes for 55 yards and 1 TD in 2025 as the TE2 behind Brenton Strange. One-year prove-it deal at $2M (up to $3M) is a depth retention. Brenton Strange is the clear TE1 and an extension candidate. TE is not a priority need — Morris maintains adequate TE2 depth.

Dallas served as RB depth in 2025 with 20 receptions, 227 yards, 1 TD and was a key special teamer. Veteran minimum re-sign. Provides depth behind Rodriguez/Tuten but does not address the bell-cow RB void meaningfully.

Dickerson was signed to the active roster during 2025 and became a rotational piece on the interior defensive line. One-year, $1.5M re-sign is depth retention. IDL/DT remains a noted pre-FA need — the Jaguars led the league in rushing defense in 2025 but struggled at times to generate interior pass-rush pressure. Dickerson does not solve that problem.
Braswell was an ERFA retained at the minimum. Played 17 games in 2025 with 14 tackles and 3 PDs. Depth CB4/special teams contributor. No needle movement on CB need.
Taylor was retained as a depth CB. No impact on starting depth chart.

Meyers was acquired via trade from the Las Vegas Raiders at the 2025 deadline and immediately transformed the offense (5-1 record after acquisition, 27 catches, 355 yards, 3 TDs over 6 games). He signed a 3yr/$60M extension in December 2025 with $40M guaranteed ($30M fully guaranteed at signing). This was a pre-free-agency retention that locked in the WR2 behind Brian Thomas Jr. through 2028. WR is not a need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Travis Etienne (1,107 rush yds, 13 total TDs) signed a 4yr/$52M deal with the Saints — a massive departure. Chris Rodriguez Jr. (2yr/$10M, $6.2M gtd) partially fills the void and enters as a potential co-starter with Bhayshul Tuten. The committee approach is credible but Rodriguez is not an elite bell-cow, lacks Etienne's receiving ability, and the Jaguars still project a 3-man backfield. RB drops from critical need (tier 1) to a meaningful need (tier 2) — still worth targeting in the mid-rounds of the draft to develop a long-term lead back.

Devin Lloyd (2nd-team All-Pro, 5 INTs, 81 tackles) signed a 3yr/$45M deal with Carolina — one of the biggest defensive departures in the offseason. Dennis Gardeck's re-sign (2yr/$6.5M) keeps a pass-rush specialist but does NOT replace Lloyd's every-down role in coverage. Internal replacement is Ventrell Miller, who has not proven himself as an NFL starter. LB escalates to tier 1 critical need — should be a priority in rounds 2-4 of the draft.

Montaric Brown's 3yr/$31.8M re-sign ($20.65M gtd) locks in the CB2 alongside Travis Hunter. Brown led the team with 12 PBUs and posted elite coverage metrics in 2025. The starting CB need is fully resolved. Greg Newsome II's departure (signed 1yr/$8M with Giants) slightly thins the depth, leaving Jarrian Jones (slot), Jourdan Lewis, and Braswell/Taylor behind the top two. CB drops from tier 2 to tier 3 — depth/developmental pick only, not a premium need.

Matt Dickerson's 1yr/$1.5M re-sign maintains rotational depth but does not address the interior pass-rush problem. The Jaguars noted pre-FA need at DT for interior pressure generation is UNCHANGED. With Arik Armstead's future uncertain (potential cut/restructure candidate) and no outside DT addition, IDL remains a tier 2 need heading into the draft. DT/IDL is commonly projected for Jacksonville in mock drafts.

Andrew Wingard's departure (to Cardinals, 1yr deal) removes a starter-level safety who had been with the franchise for 7 years. Eric Murray and Antonio Johnson start with rookie Caleb Ransaw (returning from IR) in the depth mix. The loss is real but the team has reasonable continuity at the position. Safety remains a tier 3 depth need — Wingard's specific role was primarily run-stopping and special teams and the overall unit is adequate.

Jakobi Meyers (3yr/$60M, $40M gtd, Dec. 2025 extension) joins Brian Thomas Jr. and Travis Hunter as locked-in starters through the prime years. Dyami Brown and Tim Patrick departures thin depth but don't impact the starting group. WR is not a draft priority — tier 4 luxury at best.

key_additions.headline: Team-high 12 PBUs and elite coverage metrics earn Brown a multi-year commitment as the locked-in CB2 opposite Travis Hunter
Former Coen pupil at Kentucky brought in as Etienne's replacement in a 1A/1B committee with Bhayshul Tuten
Pass-rush specialist with 25 pressures and 2.5 sacks re-signed to maintain depth after Lloyd's departure
Midseason trade acquisition locked up through 2028 after transforming the offense; $40M guaranteed signals long-term WR2 commitment

key_losses.headline: Franchise RB (1,107 rush yds, 13 total TDs in 2025) returns home to Louisiana on a $52M deal the cap-strapped Jaguars could not match
Second-team All-Pro linebacker (5 INTs, 81 tackles) leaves after a breakout 2025 season, creating Jacksonville's biggest defensive hole
Midseason trade acquisition and CB2/CB3 departs on a prove-it deal, thinning cornerback depth behind Brown and Hunter
7-year Jaguars fan favorite and starter departs, ending a beloved tenure and adding to the safety depth question
